title: Unlikely Intersections in the Irregular-singular Context
speaker: Gal Binyamini
abstract: I'll talk about some classical problems around orthogonal polynomials and harmonic analysis that fall into the "unlikely intersection" paradigm: they can be reformulated as questions about counting integers in definable sets whose algebraic parts are controlled by suitable Ax-Schanuel type theorems. Some interesting new features arise in this context: The "uniformizing maps" are more analytically involved: their study requires the steepest descent or other asymptotic methods, making the analysis more challenging than in the classical setting. Accordingly, the relevant sets are not definable in R_{an,exp} but rather in the larger o-minimal structure R_{G,exp} of multisummable functions. Functional transcendence is still controlled by differential Galois theory, but now for irregular-singular systems. This brings new concepts (exponential tori, Stokes phenomena) into the description of the weakly special subvarieties. Based on joint work with Avner Kiro and Jonathan Pila.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: O-minimal unlikely-intersection template
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames two new unlikely-intersection problems as situations where one should pull special points back to rational or integer points in definable sets and then combine Pila-Wilkie counting with functional transcendence.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal-polynomial roots as special points
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Legendre, Laguerre, and Hermite-type orthogonal polynomials, the speaker formulates common-root and Manin-Mumford-style questions by declaring coordinate equalities and polynomial roots to be the special conditions.

proof_stages.label: Fuglede problem as Bessel-zero intersections
proof_stages.summary: The orthogonality of exponentials on the unit disk is converted via the Fourier transform of the disk into the condition that pairwise distances lie among zeros of the Bessel function, yielding a special-point problem on the distance variety of four planar points and an announced sub-polynomial counting theorem.

proof_stages.label: Definable uniformizers from asymptotic expansions
proof_stages.summary: Although the Bessel function and families of orthogonal polynomials are globally oscillatory and not ordinarily definable, classical asymptotic expansions separate exponential oscillation from multisummable definable factors, producing definable maps from integer parameters to Bessel zeros and to polynomial roots.

proof_stages.label: Pila-Wilkie pullback reduction
proof_stages.summary: For a variety of interest, one pulls it back under the definable zero-parametrizing map and applies Pila-Wilkie, reducing the desired count of special points to understanding or excluding the algebraic part of the pullback.

proof_stages.label: Bessel Ax-Schanuel and weakly special relations
proof_stages.summary: A Bessel Ax-Schanuel theorem is stated and justified using differential algebraic Ax-Schanuel criteria, the sparse differential Galois group, and explicit computation of the irregular differential Galois group, leaving only the expected relations such as exponential linear dependence and x_i=±x_j.

proof_stages.label: Parameterized orthogonal-polynomial transcendence and consequences
proof_stages.summary: For Laguerre-type equations with a spectral parameter, a generic-parameter Ax-Schanuel statement is used to rule out bounded-degree algebraic blocks after specialization, and together with Galois-orbit input this yields Manin-Mumford results for families with sufficient irreducibility and partial polylogarithmic bounds for Legendre common roots.

examples.name: Zero shared by odd Legendre polynomials
examples.note: The trivial common root at zero for odd Legendre polynomials forces the common-root question to be formulated with an explicit exception.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only notes that zero is a common root for odd Legendre polynomials because they are odd functions, without computing or analyzing that case further.

passes.reason: the root 0 is the parity-fixed trivial common root of all odd Legendre polynomials, motivating the “except zero” caveat
The common root is the trivial parity-fixed zero for odd Legendre polynomials, forcing the “except zero” caveat.
The shared root is the trivial parity-forced central zero, the excluded exception to a naive no-common-root claim.

0 is the parity-fixed trivial root forced by oddness, and it is cited as the excluded shared root defeating a blanket no-common-root claim.

examples.name: Torsion and CM point analogy
examples.note: Classical Manin-Mumford/André-Oort special points are used as the model for treating polynomial roots or Bessel zeros as special points and for seeking an integer-index uniformization.
examples.engagement_reason: Torsion and CM points are used as an analogy for polynomial roots in a special-point framework, but no work is done on torsion or CM examples themselves.

examples.name: Fuglede tiling heuristic for the disk
examples.note: The unit disk’s failure to tile the plane is recalled as motivation for why one expects no large orthogonal exponential set there.
examples.engagement_reason: The disk is invoked via the Fuglede tiling motivation—saying it does not tile the plane and hence should not have an orthogonal basis—but no tiling construction or verification is carried out.

examples.name: Cosine-like oscillation of the Bessel function
examples.note: The speaker compares the Bessel function to a decaying cosine to make visible both the source of many zeros and the obstruction to ordinary definability.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the Bessel differential equation and asymptotic expansion, identifies the oscillatory exponential terms, and uses them to construct a definable parametrization of Bessel zeros.

examples.name: Three-point Bessel-zero configuration
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that three points are possible by choosing three Bessel zeros satisfying the triangle inequality and realizing them as mutual distances.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to whether three points are possible, the speaker explains how to realize a three-point configuration by choosing three Bessel zeros satisfying the triangle inequality and forming the corresponding triangle.

passes.reason: a three-point configuration with Bessel-zero distances achieves the conjectural maximal size below four rather than refuting it
A three-point Bessel-zero distance configuration is the tight possible case just below the conjecturally forbidden four-point case.
A three-point configuration with Bessel-zero distances realizes the largest size allowed by the proposed '<4' bound, showing sharpness rather than failure.
